title: Equilibrating continuous-variable open quantum systems using stochastic classical trajectories in path-integral space
authors: William H. D. Moore, Stuart C. Althorpe
abstract: Beyond the weak-coupling limit, open quantum systems equilibrate to a highly entangled thermal state. For continuous-variable systems, this state can be written explicitly as an imaginary-time phase-space path integral, in which the positions are directly entangled with the bath, and the momenta are correlated with the positions through a phase term. Here, we ask to what extent this state can be reached by propagating stochastic classical trajectories in path-integral phase space. Surprisingly, we find that the trajectories equilibrate to the exact quantum equilibrium state, recovering the purely imaginary momentum-position correlation in the phase term. The trajectories are generated using a recently derived Matsubara generalized Langevin equation, which produces the imaginary correlations by evolving the stochastic variables into the complex plane. This makes the dynamics numerically unstable, but we are nonetheless able to demonstrate the equilibration of a quartic oscillator coupled to a white-noise bath. These unexpected findings could lead to new approximate methodologies for simulating continuous-variable open quantum systems.
summary: This paper investigates simulating the thermalization of continuous-variable open quantum systems using stochastic classical trajectories within path-integral phase space. The authors demonstrate that these trajectories correctly equilibrate to the exact quantum equilibrium state by solving a derived Fokker-Planck Equation. This offers a novel, albeit numerically challenging, theoretical framework for modeling complex open quantum dynamics.
main_problem: The core problem is determining if stochastic classical trajectories evolving in path-integral phase space can correctly equilibrate an open quantum system to its exact thermal equilibrium state.
main_result: The study finds that the stochastic trajectories generated via a Matsubara generalized Langevin equation successfully equilibrate to the exact quantum equilibrium state, recovering necessary imaginary momentum-position correlations.
method: The authors employ path-integral formulations and derive a corresponding Fokker-Planck Equation (FPE) from a generalized Langevin equation (GLE). They then analyze the stationary solution of this FPE.
model_or_system: The system is an open quantum system modeled by coupling a system Hamiltonian ($\hat{H}_s$) to a bath ($\hat{H}_b$) via $\hat{H}_{sb}$. The equilibrium state is analyzed using continuous-variable phase space path integrals in imaginary time.
key_observables: The stationary solution of the FPE ($ho_{stat}$), which must marginalize to the known quantum equilibrium distribution ($ho_{eq}$).
important_parameters: Temperature ($ eta$), system frequency/potential ($V(q)$), and bath spectral density (e.g., Debye-Drude).
main_assumptions: The method relies on mapping non-Markovian dynamics to a Markovian FPE using auxiliary variables, although the resulting GLE itself is numerically unstable when evolved into the complex plane.
figures_summary: Figures show convergence of moments (like $\langle Q^2_n(t) angle$) towards the quantum Boltzmann distribution moments, confirming equilibration.
paper_structure: The paper develops the path-integral formulation for open systems, derives a generalized Langevin equation (GLE), maps this to a Markovian FPE using auxiliary variables, and finally solves/analyzes the stationary solution of the FPE to confirm agreement with known quantum equilibrium results.
why_interesting: This work provides an alternative, trajectory-based methodology—stochastic classical paths in phase space—to simulate complex open quantum dynamics, which is valuable for developing new approximate simulation techniques beyond standard master equation approaches.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
